transcript.sec: Starting on Twitter and dealing with criticism

turns.en: So we might as well participate. And so I started being really active on Twitter specifically because that's where journalists really lived at the time. And I thought it would show some humility to show up on their turf, so to speak. My Twitter ended up being the darkest place in my life because I just followed all of our biggest critics. That's not a dig on Twitter. That was just what I did. And that's where it started and it kind of slowly built from there. For better, for worse, we've become a really important part of daily life for a lot of people. We touch a lot of people. We have a lot of responsibility and there's a lot of change, and with change there's going to be anxiety and stress and scrutiny. We've made great decisions. We've made mistakes. We've been criticized for things that I think we've been criticized unfairly. We've been criticized fairly. And so we just need to accept that this debate is gonna happen broadly. So I just think it's better for us to talk about it and just be clear about what we're doing, why we're doing it, what the trade-offs are. If people disagree, that's okay. We're not necessarily winning over friends when we talk about what we do. But I think over the long run, people are fundamentally more afraid of things that they don't understand and about things where people are more secretive and less accessible. And so I've tried to show up in an accessible and authentic way. And I've made mistakes and I have enjoyed it at times and hated it at other times. But that's kind of how it started. There was also kind of a fun debate in Mark's senior leadership team a long time ago where we were just talking about how we're a social media company where we had a very conventional approach to communication, like press releases, and why don't we just use our platform. So I was not in that debate, but I stuck myself into that debate, trying to mediate it. And I think that was also a reason why I ended up getting sucked in, because Mark was like, "All right, well, let's see. Why don't you try and see how it goes?"
turns.zh: 所以我们不妨参与进去。于是我开始在 Twitter 上非常活跃，特别是因为当时记者们都在那里。我觉得出现在他们的地盘上能显示一些谦逊。我的 Twitter 最终成了我生命中最黑暗的地方，因为我只关注了我们最大的批评者。这不是在贬低 Twitter，只是我自己的做法。事情就是这样开始的，然后慢慢发展起来。无论好坏，我们已经成为很多人日常生活中非常重要的一部分。我们影响着很多人，肩负着很多责任，也面临着很多变化，而变化会带来焦虑、压力和审视。我们做过很棒的决定，也犯过错误。我们被批评过，有些我觉得不公平，有些则很公平。所以我们只需要接受这场辩论会广泛发生。我认为我们最好还是谈论它，清楚地说明我们在做什么、为什么做、以及有哪些权衡。如果人们不同意，那也没关系。我们谈论我们所做的事情时，不一定能赢得朋友。但我认为从长远来看，人们从根本上更害怕他们不理解的事情，以及那些更隐秘、更难接近的事情。所以我一直努力以可接近和真实的方式出现。我犯过错误，有时享受其中，有时又讨厌它。但这就是开始的方式。很久以前，在马克的高级领导团队中也有过一场有趣的辩论，我们讨论的是，作为一家社交媒体公司，我们的沟通方式非常传统，比如新闻稿，为什么我们不直接使用自己的平台。我本来不在那场辩论中，但我把自己插了进去，试图调解。我认为这也是我最终被卷入的原因，因为马克说：“好吧，我们看看。你为什么不试试看效果如何？”

turns.en: What's something that helps you deal with the hate that flows at you every time you say something that people disagree with?
turns.zh: 有什么东西能帮助你应对每次你说出人们不同意的话时涌来的仇恨？

turns.en: You try to put it in perspective, right? You know, so it started with I did the redesign of Newsfeed in 2009. We launched it, March of 2009 for Facebook. I was a designer. I was an IC designer, front, entry-level designer. And the first comment that came in was something pretty derogatory. I think it was homophobic and anti-semitic, you know, it was just like literally we're all sitting there. We launched this thing and we're just looking at the stream of comments and it's like the first one and it was specifically about they don't know me but it was like what expletive, censored, censored designed this and I was like oh it was me. And I was devastated. I was a 25-year-old kid and I don't know, I thought about it and I came to this idea that if you spent 30, 40, 50 minutes a day at your desk and you organized your photos there and you wrote letters to your friends there and you read there and then I just came and I rearranged your desk and I didn't tell you, I didn't warn you. I didn't even explain why. Like you would be pissed and that would be reasonable. And that was what was happening just, you know, with millions of people. So I try to put things in perspective. And then I try to step away from it. Get time with my kids, get time outside. There are months where it's really not hard at all and there are months where it's really, really grinds on me.
turns.zh: 你试着把它放在视角中看待，对吧？你知道，这始于 2009 年我重新设计 Newsfeed 的时候。我们在 2009 年 3 月为 Facebook 推出了它。我当时是一名设计师，一名 IC 设计师，前端，入门级设计师。第一条评论非常贬低。我想是恐同和反犹太的，你知道，我们所有人都坐在那里。我们发布了这个东西，正在看评论流，第一条评论就是关于——他们不认识我，但说的是“哪个该死的、被屏蔽的、被屏蔽的设计了这个”，我心想，哦，是我。我崩溃了。我当时是个 25 岁的孩子，我不知道，我想了想，得出了一个想法：如果你每天在办公桌前花 30、40、50 分钟，在那里整理照片、给朋友写信、阅读，然后我过来重新整理了你的桌子，没有告诉你，没有警告你，甚至没有解释为什么。你会很生气，而且那是合理的。而当时发生的事情正是如此，只不过涉及数百万人。所以我试着把事情放在视角中看待。然后我试着抽身离开。花时间陪孩子，去户外。有些月份一点也不难熬，有些月份则真的让我很煎熬。

transcript.sec: The video redesign backlash and lessons learned

turns.en: Along those lines, there's a famous kind of reversal when you redesign the feed into this kind of video scrolly experience. There's this whole protest. The world protested.
turns.zh: 顺着这个思路，有一个著名的反转事件，当你把信息流重新设计成这种视频滚动体验时。全世界都在抗议。

turns.en: Yeah, that was pretty rough.
turns.zh: 是的，那相当艰难。

turns.en: What was kind of like, okay, wow, we're actually not right and we should go back. What was kind of what helped you decide, okay, let's change course.
turns.zh: 当时是什么感觉，比如，好吧，哇，我们其实不对，应该回头。是什么帮助你决定，好吧，我们改变方向。

turns.en: So, actually that one got conflated with three or four things. We had a redesign of feed that went to the video viewer that was a test to 4% of users on iOS. It was not going to roll out. It was just an early test to get some sense and feedback on the idea. We were also leaning into Reels a lot. We were also leaning into recommendations, so posts from accounts you don't follow a lot. And there were also creators who were upset about the fact that their reach was going down and they were blaming ranking changes on that. Those four things got all conflated. We had some pretty big-name creators publicly slap us. Then the press covered that creator sort of backlash which then got more creators doing it. So we ended up with this little bit of like a multiplier effect or echo between the creator community and the press back and forth, but we were never going to launch that. That was an early test. We knew it needed work. We actually have continued to grow video and invest in creative tools and invest in ranking and invest in recommendations and that's driven most of our growth in the years since. I think we were pushed, well I don't know, doesn't feel like, but like it, I think we were... I think my real takeaway wasn't that we should have not tested that design necessarily. I think we could have done a bunch of things better to explain and maybe move a little fast, move a little slower. I think we were just pushing things a little bit too fast. And when you are responsible for a platform like Instagram, you need to be reasonable and realistic about how much you can evolve it. Now, I would much rather have backlashes like that every couple years or continue to evolve and continue to stay relevant than the alternative, which would have been like we didn't have video, we didn't have DMs, we didn't have Stories, we didn't have ranking, and we wouldn't be having this podcast right now. But the cost of leaning in is that you're going to occasionally make a mistake and you're going to definitely pay for it.
turns.zh: 实际上，那件事和三四个事情混在了一起。我们对信息流进行了重新设计，变成了视频查看器，那是对 iOS 上 4% 用户的一个测试。它不会全面推出，只是一个早期测试，用来了解这个想法的效果和反馈。我们也在大力投入 Reels，大力投入推荐，也就是来自你不关注账号的帖子。还有一些创作者因为他们的触达下降而不满，他们把这归咎于排名变化。这四件事全部混在了一起。我们有一些大牌创作者公开抨击我们。然后媒体报道了创作者的反弹，这又引发了更多创作者加入。所以我们最终在创作者社区和媒体之间形成了一种乘数效应或回声，但我们从未打算推出那个设计。那只是一个早期测试。我们知道它需要改进。实际上，我们后来继续发展视频，投资创作工具，投资排名和推荐，这推动了此后几年我们的大部分增长。我认为我们被推着走，好吧，我不确定，感觉不像，但我觉得……我真正的收获并不是我们不应该测试那个设计。我认为我们本可以做得更好来解释，也许快一点或慢一点。我认为我们只是推进得太快了。当你负责像 Instagram 这样的平台时，你需要合理且现实地考虑你能进化多少。现在，我宁愿每隔几年经历一次那样的反弹，继续进化并保持相关性，也不愿选择另一种情况，即我们没有视频、没有私信、没有故事、没有排名，我们现在也不会做这个播客。但积极投入的代价是，你偶尔会犯错，而且你肯定会为此付出代价。

turns.en: It's interesting how running experiments now is like very risky for companies at your scale. One person spots it and they're like, "Oh shit." You kind of need to have a press. You don't need to be proactive about communicating it, but you need to have a calm strategy. Like we can't for any design change or any test that could be controversial.
turns.zh: 有趣的是，对于你们这种规模的公司来说，现在运行实验风险很大。一个人发现了，他们就会说“哦，糟了”。你需要有媒体策略。你不必主动沟通，但你需要有一个冷静的策略。比如，我们不能对任何可能有争议的设计变更或测试掉以轻心。

transcript.secZh: 视频重新设计的反弹与教训

turns.en: We talk about it beforehand and be like, 'Okay, not if it leaks, when it leaks, what are we saying?' Should we talk proactively or reactively? Either way, what's the message? Because you can't launch something to three billion people and not test it first, but you can't test something at our scale and not expect people to cover it. So you have to be ready to talk about it before you even know you want to launch it. It makes the development cycle more complicated than it used to be.
turns.zh: 我们事先会讨论，比如‘好吧，不是会不会泄露，而是什么时候泄露，我们该怎么说？’我们是主动谈还是被动回应？不管怎样，信息是什么？因为你不能在没有测试的情况下向 30 亿人发布产品，但以我们的规模测试，又不可能指望没人报道。所以你必须在你甚至不确定要不要发布之前，就准备好怎么说。这让开发周期比以前更复杂了。

turns.en: Yeah. The head of growth at Anthropic launched an experiment with pricing and it just went crazy on Twitter. He's like, 'Hold that, 1% of people were just trending stuff.'
turns.zh: 是啊。Anthropic 的增长负责人搞了个定价实验，结果在 Twitter 上炸了锅。他说，‘等等，1% 的人就在刷趋势了。’

turns.en: Pricing particularly is one you've got to be real careful with. I've learned, we've all learned these lessons. We should all share notes more. That's what I think.
turns.zh: 定价尤其要非常小心。我学到了，我们都学到了这些教训。我觉得我们应该多交流经验。

turns.en: Here's one not to: how to avoid the internet hating you for the day.
turns.zh: 这里有一条反面教材：如何避免让互联网恨你一整天。

turns.en: Yeah. I'm happy to talk to that more.
turns.zh: 是啊。我很乐意多聊聊这个。

turns.en: I think he's all right. Okay, I'm going to take us to two recurring corners on the podcast: Fail Corner and Hot Seat Corner. Fail Corner: what's something that you worked on that was a huge failure that helped you become better?
turns.zh: 我觉得他还行。好了，我要带大家进入播客的两个固定环节：失败角落和热椅角落。失败角落：你做过什么巨大的失败，让你变得更好？

transcript.sec: Fail Corner: Facebook Home and early Reels

turns.en: Oh, a bunch. So I'll give you two maybe. Before Instagram, my first project as a PM was on a project called Facebook Home, which was a sort of fork of Android at the operating system level and a piece of hardware with HTC. It was a spectacular failure. I learned way more in that year and a half than in any year in my career, I think. I was just a design manager before that. I declared myself a PM because the PM on the project quit, and I just threw myself head first into understanding carriers, OEMs, certification, as well as Android and operating systems. I learned a ton. I'm happy I brought that project to an end because it had been going on for a long time. Sometimes the best thing you can do is execute an idea that doesn't have market fit well just to decide whether or not the idea was a good idea in the first place. Another big mistake I made during my Instagram tenure was the first version of Reels was built on top of Stories. Stories had a ton of momentum. This was 2019, I think, and we were trying to build Reels into Stories because we were trying to build into the thing that was growing the fastest. But it was not a strong foundation. The read-through rate on Stories is relatively low. There are way more Stories than most people have time to consume. So most of the Reels were never seen, and then they disappeared. If we had the version of Reels that we launched in mid-2020 in the summer of 2019, I don't think TikTok is as big as it is now. Because when they really took off was when the pandemic hit, and a bunch of people had a lot of time at home and were looking for a little bit of joy and were totally fine with their phone having sound on. If you look at the numbers, 2020 is when they exploded, and we were out of position. On one hand, I'm a designer. I'm trying to not add new things to the product. I'm trying to extend existing primitives, and that was the idea. On the other hand, I was wrong, and it's a pretty big fork in the road if you just look at the overall business over the last eight years.
turns.zh: 哦，有很多。我给你讲两个吧。在 Instagram 之前，我作为产品经理的第一个项目叫 Facebook Home，它有点像 Android 在操作系统层面的一个分支，还和 HTC 合作做了一款硬件。那是一次惨败。我在那一年半里学到的东西，可能比我职业生涯中任何一年都多。在那之前我只是个设计经理。我自封为产品经理，因为项目上的产品经理辞职了，我就一头扎进去，去理解运营商、OEM、认证，还有 Android 和操作系统。我学到了很多。我很高兴我结束了那个项目，因为它已经进行了很久。有时候，最好的做法就是去执行一个没有市场契合度的想法，只是为了判断这个想法本身到底好不好。我在 Instagram 期间犯的另一个大错误是，第一版 Reels 是建立在 Stories 之上的。Stories 当时势头很猛。那大概是 2019 年，我们想把 Reels 塞进 Stories，因为我们想把它建在增长最快的东西上。但那不是一个坚实的基础。Stories 的阅读完成率相对较低。Stories 的数量远远超过大多数人能看完的。所以大多数 Reels 根本没人看，然后就消失了。如果我们能在 2019 年夏天就推出 2020 年中那个版本的 Reels，我觉得 TikTok 不会像现在这么大。因为 TikTok 真正起飞是在疫情爆发的时候，很多人待在家里，想找点乐子，而且完全不介意手机开着声音。你看数据，2020 年他们爆发了，而我们没跟上。一方面，我是个设计师，我不想给产品加太多新东西，我想扩展已有的基础元素，这就是当时的想法。另一方面，我错了，从过去八年的整体业务来看，这算是一个很大的分岔路口。

turns.en: We created a lot of economic opportunity in the world allowing TikTok to grow.
turns.zh: 我们给世界创造了大量经济机会，让 TikTok 得以成长。

turns.en: That's good. I'm glad they exist.
turns.zh: 那很好。我很高兴他们存在。

turns.en: Okay, final question. I'm curious about your screen time policy with your kids. I know you have three kids. There's a lot of concern these days about Instagram not being great for people, not for kids. A lot of tech executives don't let their kids use devices while they're building the product. As head of Instagram, how do you think about screen time with your kids?
turns.zh: 好了，最后一个问题。我很好奇你对孩子的屏幕时间管理。我知道你有三个孩子。现在很多人担心 Instagram 对人们不好，对孩子也不好。很多科技高管在开发产品的同时，却不让孩子用设备。作为 Instagram 的负责人，你怎么看待孩子的屏幕时间？

turns.en: The key thing for me is boundaries. It's also about education and having conversations with them. But my kids are too young to use social media. They're 10, 8, and 6. But they each have an iPad. They have to earn their time. They have different ways to earn that time. It's usually about sitting down to do homework three times for half an hour each, which gets them a total of 90 minutes on the weekend. Then they can use that time on the weekend. You have to set that boundary where it's not just they ask for it and you give it to them. I think that matters a lot. Then I'm pretty opinionated about what they do on it. I approve what apps they have. I think parents should be approving what apps kids download onto their devices. We've been advocating for this at a policy level for a long time at Meta. I think those things help a lot. There are some exceptions. One is planes. It's just about surviving. I don't know if you've ever—for those of you who are parents. It's like, 'All right, you know, we're flying a 10-hour flight or eight-hour flight. Yeah, just get through it.' The other one I'm starting to experiment with my 10-year-old is schools. I'm pretty supportive of no phone in classrooms. That's happening more and more. I think that's probably good for education. I also know in the world of AI that there's concern about kids using AI and not learning critical thinking skills. That's a valid concern. But I also worry about kids not learning how to leverage AI and then being at a disadvantage. So that's a balance. I think you need both. With my eldest, we started vibe coding recently together. He loves video games. So I said, 'All right, let's make a video game.' He's made this 19-level platformer game that looks like an 8-bit version of Super Mario from when I was a kid. But each level has its own theme, its own types of monsters. There's a store where you can buy different skins or weapons. It's unbelievable what a 10-year-old who still types with three fingers can do with just a couple hours of sitting together. But that's more of a 'I want you to learn how to make things. I want you to be thinking, not just playing games, and I'm going to sit with you and we're going to do this together.' To me, these are the things that matter.
turns.zh: 对我来说，关键是设定界限。还有教育，以及和他们沟通。但我的孩子还太小，不能用社交媒体。他们分别是 10 岁、8 岁和 6 岁。不过他们每人都有一个 iPad。他们得自己挣屏幕时间。有不同的方式去挣。通常是坐下来做三次作业，每次半小时，这样周末就能得到总共 90 分钟。然后他们可以在周末用这些时间。你得设定界限，不能他们一要你就给。我觉得这很重要。然后我对他们在上面做什么很有主见。我批准他们安装哪些应用。我认为家长应该批准孩子下载到设备上的应用。我们在 Meta 从政策层面倡导这个已经很久了。我觉得这些很有帮助。也有一些例外。一个是飞机上。那纯粹是为了熬过去。我不知道你有没有——如果你是家长的话。就像，‘好吧，我们要飞 10 个小时或 8 个小时。嗯，熬过去就行。’另一个我开始在我 10 岁孩子身上尝试的是学校。我非常支持课堂上不用手机。这种情况越来越普遍。我认为这对教育可能有好处。我也知道在 AI 领域，有人担心孩子用 AI 会学不到批判性思维技能。这个担忧有道理。但我也担心孩子学不会利用 AI，然后处于劣势。所以这是个平衡。我觉得两者都需要。我和大儿子最近开始一起 vibe coding。他喜欢电子游戏。所以我说，‘好，我们做个游戏吧。’他做了一个 19 关的平台游戏，看起来像我小时候玩的 8 位版超级马里奥。但每一关都有自己的主题和怪物类型。还有一个商店，可以买不同的皮肤或武器。一个 10 岁、还用三个手指打字的孩子，只要一起坐几个小时就能做出这种东西，真是难以置信。但这更像是‘我想让你学会如何创造东西。我想让你思考，不只是玩游戏，我会和你一起坐在这里，我们一起做。’对我来说，这些才是重要的。

transcript.secZh: 与孩子的屏幕时间政策

turns.en: Boundaries, scoping it down to the activities you think are healthy for your kid. Every kid is different. But I do think you want your kids to be digitally literate, AI literate, because I think if they're not, they're going to be at a disadvantage. But you also don't want it to be a free-for-all.
turns.zh: 设定界限，把范围缩小到你认为对孩子健康的活动上。每个孩子都不一样。但我确实认为你希望你的孩子具备数字素养和 AI 素养，因为我觉得如果他们不具备，就会处于劣势。但你也不希望它变成毫无约束的混乱局面。

turns.en: This is selfishly useful for me as a I have a three-year-old and I'm trying to figure all this stuff out. So, this is useful.
turns.zh: 这对我个人来说很有用，因为我有一个三岁的孩子，我正在努力搞清楚这些事情。所以，这很有帮助。

turns.en: Oh, yeah. No, we need to figure out a strategy.
turns.zh: 哦，是的。不，我们需要制定一个策略。

turns.en: It's amazing. It's a thing. And you're not that far off. You're really just not that far off. It's going to happen in a couple years.
turns.zh: 这太棒了。这确实是个事。而且你离得不远。你真的离得不远。再过几年就会实现。

turns.en: Five coding next year. Let's do it.
turns.zh: 明年五岁开始编程。就这么办。

turns.en: I couldn't believe I tried to do it six months ago and it just totally didn't work. And then now with newer models, it's been amazing.
turns.zh: 我简直不敢相信，六个月前我试过，但完全不行。而现在有了更新的模型，效果惊人。

turns.en: What's their platform of choice? Are they a Claude Code person?
turns.zh: 他们选择的平台是什么？是用 Claude Code 的人吗？

turns.en: Yeah, my 10-year-old is using Claude Code right now. But we will see. We'll see how that goes.
turns.zh: 是的，我十岁的孩子现在正在用 Claude Code。但我们会看看。我们会看看情况如何。

turns.en: Adam, I'm going to let you go. Thank you so much for being here. You're just such a gem of a person. It's just so obvious how clear, like how authentic you are and just like how deeply you think about everything. So I really appreciate you being here.
turns.zh: Adam，我要放你走了。非常感谢你来到这里。你真是个难得的好人。很明显，你多么清晰、多么真诚，而且你对每件事思考得多么深入。所以我真的很感激你能来。

turns.en: I appreciate you bringing me on. Been a fan for a long time. It's nice to finally get to have a conversation.
turns.zh: 感谢你邀请我。我当你的粉丝很久了。终于能聊一聊，真好。

turns.en: I appreciate that. I really appreciate that. Let me just ask you this final question to ask everyone. What's a way that listeners can be useful to you?
turns.zh: 我很感激。真的很感激。让我问你最后一个问题，我问每个人的。听众们能怎么帮到你？

turns.en: I just think you don't even have to tell this to other people, but just remember that this world and technology is complicated and there are almost always tradeoffs. And you can totally disagree with the decisions I or we make. But just remember that we are people here trying to make these decisions, trying to do the best we can. And I actually do invite the criticism and the critique and the feedback, but know that none of these contentious debates are nearly as simple as most people pretend to make them out to be.
turns.zh: 我只是觉得，你甚至不必把这个告诉别人，但请记住，这个世界和技术是复杂的，几乎总是存在权衡。你完全可以不同意我或我们做出的决定。但请记住，我们也是人，在这里努力做出这些决定，尽力做到最好。我实际上确实欢迎批评、指正和反馈，但要知道，这些有争议的辩论没有一个是像大多数人假装的那样简单。

turns.en: Wise words, Adam, thank you so much for being here.
turns.zh: 金玉良言，Adam，非常感谢你来到这里。

turns.en: Pleasure. Thank you, Lenny.
turns.zh: 荣幸之至。谢谢你，Lenny。

turns.en: Bye, everyone. Thank you so much for listening. If you found this valuable, you can subscribe to the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or your favorite podcast app. Also, please consider giving us a rating or leaving a review as that really helps other listeners find the podcast. You can find all past episodes or learn more about the show at lennispodcast.com. See you in the next episode.
turns.zh: 再见，各位。非常感谢你们的收听。如果你觉得这期节目有价值，可以在 Apple Podcasts、Spotify 或你最喜欢的播客应用上订阅本节目。另外，请考虑给我们评分或写评论，这真的能帮助其他听众找到这个播客。你可以在 lennispodcast.com 找到所有往期节目或了解更多关于本节目的信息。下期节目再见。
